questions.context:
@maya_writes

Okay, I have to gush about last night's Open Mic at the Roastery Café — easily the best campus event I've been to all semester!! 🎤

The space only holds about forty people, so it filled up fast, but the $3 entry covered a free hot drink, which honestly made it worth it. There were poets, a jazz duo, and one guy whose stand-up had the whole room crying with laughter. The host kept things moving and even coaxed a few nervous first-timers onto the stage.

The next one is the first Thursday of December, same place, 7 p.m. If you want a spot, get there early — they don't take reservations and the line was out the door by 6:45. Bring a friend and maybe something to perform! Who's coming with me? 🙌
questions.stem: According to the post, what did the entry fee include?

options.A: A reserved seat near the stage
options.B: A free hot drink
options.C: A ticket to a future show
options.D: A discount on the café menu

questions.context:
Subject: Introducing the new Campus Tool Library

Dear residents,

We're excited to announce that the Campus Tool Library is now open in the basement of Hartwell Hall. Instead of buying equipment you will use only once, you can now borrow it for free with your student card.

Our shelves include power drills, hand tools, sewing machines, camping gear, and even a few musical instruments. Most items can be borrowed for up to one week. Larger tools, such as ladders and tile cutters, are limited to three days so that everyone gets a turn.

Please return items clean and on time; a $2 daily fee applies to late returns. If a tool is damaged, just let us know — honest reports help us keep everything in good working order.

Stop by Monday through Friday, 1 to 6 p.m.

Best,
The Tool Library Team
questions.stem: According to the email, how long can most items be borrowed?

options.A: For a single day only
options.B: For up to one week
options.C: For up to three days
options.D: For the entire semester

questions.subtopic: campus bookstore moving to a new building
questions.context: Attention, students. Please note that the campus bookstore will be relocating to its new home on the ground floor of the Lewis Student Center. The current location in Foster Hall will close after this Friday, and the new store opens the following Monday at 9 a.m. The new space will offer expanded hours and a small café. questions.subtopic: student art market in the quad
questions.context: Hello, everyone! The Student Arts Council is excited to announce our first Spring Art Market, happening this Saturday from 11 a.m. to 4 p.m. on the main quad. Student artists will sell handmade jewelry, prints, ceramics, and much more, with all proceeds going directly to the creators. Admission is completely free, so bring your friends and some cash, since not every booth can accept cards. In case of rain, the market will simply move indoors to the Union ballroom. A few tables are still available for sellers who sign up by Thursday afternoon.

questions.subtopic: the principle behind how a lever multiplies force
questions.context: Let's look at one of the oldest and simplest machines humans ever invented: the lever. A lever is just a rigid bar that pivots around a fixed point we call the fulcrum. What makes it so useful is that it lets you multiply the force you apply. Think about trying to lift a heavy rock by hand. Nearly impossible. But slide a sturdy bar under that rock, rest the bar on a small stone near it, and push down on the far end, and suddenly you can raise the rock with ease. So how does this actually work? The secret is distance from the fulcrum. The force you apply and the load you are lifting sit on opposite sides of the pivot. If your end of the bar is much farther from the fulcrum than the rock is, your push gets amplified. A force applied three times farther out produces roughly three times the lifting force at the load. This relationship is what we call mechanical advantage. Now, here's the catch, and it's an important one. You don't get this extra force for free. To lift the rock just a few centimeters, your end of the bar has to travel a much longer distance. The lever trades distance for force. You move farther but push less hard, while the load moves only a little, yet with great force. Energy, in other words, is conserved. Levers don't create energy; they simply let us rearrange how effort is applied, which is exactly why they show up everywhere, from scissors to wheelbarrows.
questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How a lever multiplies the force a person applies
options.B: The history of simple machines used in building
options.C: Why heavy rocks are so difficult to lift by hand
options.D: How mechanical devices create new energy

questions.context: Today I want to introduce you to one of the toughest animals on Earth, and it's one you've almost certainly never seen: the tardigrade, often nicknamed the water bear. These creatures are tiny, less than a millimeter long, and they live in moss, soil, and sediment all over the planet. What makes them remarkable is their ability to survive conditions that would kill almost any other animal. When their environment dries out, tardigrades do something extraordinary. They pull in their legs, expel most of the water from their bodies, and curl into a shriveled, barrel-shaped form called a tun. In this state, their metabolism nearly stops. We call this cryptobiosis, essentially a pause button on life. A tardigrade can remain like this for years, then revive within minutes once water returns. How do they avoid the damage that usually comes with drying out? As they lose water, tardigrades produce special proteins that turn the inside of their cells into a kind of glass. This glassy state holds delicate cell structures in place so they don't collapse or break apart. Think of it like packing fragile dishes in foam instead of an empty box. This same protection helps tardigrades endure extreme cold, intense radiation, and even the vacuum of space, where some have famously survived. Now, I should be careful here: this does not mean tardigrades are indestructible. In their active, hydrated state, they are actually quite fragile. Their extraordinary toughness depends entirely on first entering that dormant tun form.
questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How tardigrades reproduce in moss and soil
options.B: How tardigrades survive extremely harsh conditions
options.C: Why most animals cannot live without water
options.D: How scientists first discovered tardigrades in space

questions.subtopic: the peak-end rule in how people remember experiences
questions.context: Have you ever noticed that the way you remember an experience can be quite different from how it actually felt moment to moment? Psychologists have a name for this: the peak-end rule. The idea is that when we look back on an event, we don't average all our feelings across the whole thing. Instead, our memory is shaped mostly by two moments: the most intense point, the peak, and how the experience ended. A famous study by the psychologist Daniel Kahneman showed this clearly. Patients undergoing an uncomfortable medical procedure rated their pain in real time. Afterward, what shaped their overall memory wasn't how long the discomfort lasted. Surprisingly, a longer procedure that ended with milder discomfort was remembered as less unpleasant than a shorter one that finished at a painful peak. The ending carried enormous weight. Notice what this means. Duration barely registers in our memory, a quirk researchers call duration neglect. Two hours of mild annoyance followed by a pleasant finish can be recalled more fondly than twenty minutes that ended badly. This has real practical value. A vacation that ends on a wonderful final day will be remembered warmly, even if the middle was ordinary. A great meal ruined by a rude farewell at the door may leave a sour memory. So if you are designing any kind of experience, pay special attention to the high point and especially to the ending. Next time, I want to look at how businesses deliberately use this principle to shape customer satisfaction.
questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How medical procedures cause lasting pain
options.B: Why longer experiences always feel more tiring
options.C: How the peak and the ending shape our memories
options.D: How Daniel Kahneman became a famous researcher

questions.subtopic: how geologists use ice cores to reconstruct ancient climates
questions.context: Imagine being able to sample the air that animals breathed eight hundred thousand years ago. Believe it or not, geologists can do something very close to that, using ice cores. An ice core is a long cylinder of ice drilled out from a glacier or a polar ice sheet, and it is one of our most valuable records of ancient climate. Here's the basic idea. Each year, snow falls on places like Antarctica and Greenland, and it doesn't fully melt. New snow buries the old, compressing it into layers of ice. Like tree rings, these layers stack up year after year, so the deeper you drill, the further back in time you go. A single core can span hundreds of thousands of years. But the real treasure is what's trapped inside. As snow compacts into ice, it seals tiny bubbles of air. Each bubble is a genuine sample of the ancient atmosphere, telling us how much carbon dioxide and methane it contained. That is how we know greenhouse gas levels today are far higher than at any point in those records. The ice itself holds another clue. The water molecules contain different forms, or isotopes, of oxygen, and the ratio between them depends on how cold it was when the snow fell. By measuring that ratio, scientists can estimate past temperatures with surprising precision. Together, the trapped air and the isotopes let researchers reconstruct both the temperature and the atmosphere of the distant past, layer by frozen layer.
questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: Why glaciers form in cold polar regions
options.B: How carbon dioxide warms the atmosphere
options.C: How tree rings record a tree's age
options.D: How ice cores reveal ancient climate conditions

questions.subtopic: two students chatting about a part-time job one just started at a warehouse
questions.context:
Woman: Hey, you started that warehouse packing job, right? How's it going?
Man: Honestly, it's exhausting. I'm on my feet for eight hours, and the shifts are mostly late at night.
Woman: Late nights? Doesn't that wreck your class schedule?
Man: That's the thing. I keep dozing off in my morning lecture, and my grades are starting to slip.
Woman: That sounds rough. Maybe ask your supervisor about switching to weekend shifts?
Man: Yeah, I think I have to. The money's good, but it's not worth failing a course over.
questions.stem: What are the speakers mainly discussing?

options.A: The drawbacks of the man's new job
options.B: How to find a part-time job on campus
options.C: The man's decision to drop a course
options.D: A disagreement about working night shifts

questions.subtopic: two friends sharing cooking tips while grocery shopping
questions.context:
Man: These tomatoes look great. I'm finally going to try making that pasta sauce.
Woman: Oh, nice! Quick tip: add a pinch of sugar to balance the acidity. It makes a huge difference.
Man: Sugar? In a tomato sauce? I never would've guessed.
Woman: Trust me. And let it simmer on low for at least an hour. Don't rush it.
Man: An hour, okay. I usually get impatient and crank up the heat.
Woman: That's the mistake. High heat turns it bitter. Low and slow is the real secret.
questions.stem: What does the woman suggest adding to the sauce?

options.A: A handful of fresh basil
options.B: A small amount of sugar
options.C: A splash of olive oil
options.D: A pinch of black pepper

questions.subtopic: two friends deciding whether to buy concert tickets now or wait
questions.context:
Man: Did you see the band we both like is playing here next month?
Woman: Yes! But the tickets are kind of pricey right now. Should we wait a bit?
Man: I've heard prices sometimes drop closer to the date, if a show doesn't sell out.
Woman: True, but this band is really popular. It could easily sell out within days.
Man: Good point. I'd rather pay a little more than miss it completely.
Woman: Same here. Let's just grab them tonight before they're gone.

options.A: She is not free on that night
options.B: She does not really like the band
options.C: She has already seen them perform
options.D: The tickets are expensive at the moment

questions.subtopic: two students comparing how they get to campus
questions.context:
Woman: You bike to campus every day, right? Isn't that exhausting?
Man: Not really. It's faster than the bus, and I skip the crowded stops.
Woman: I take the bus, but lately it's always packed and running late.
Man: That's why I switched. Though when it rains, I admit I really miss the dry bus.
Woman: Yeah, the weather's the catch. Maybe I'll try walking on nice days.
Man: Walking's great if you have the time. It's about thirty minutes from the dorms.

options.A: Different ways of getting to campus
options.B: A problem with the campus bus schedule
options.C: How to stay fit while commuting
options.D: Why the man dislikes biking
